results.requirement: Validates the complete FedRAMP Minimum Assessment Scope (MAS) covering machine resources, human resources, and process resources. This rule enforces Structural Boundaries (VPC ID) for compute assets, validates peripheral assets (S3, RDS, ALB), enumerates privileged human access (IAM), and confirms governance processes exist.
results.implementation_summary: Meridian maintains a single-VPC architecture with all production resources (5 EC2 instances, 1 RDS database, 11 S3 buckets, 1 ALB) explicitly enumerated and validated daily. Human resources are inventoried via IAM user and role enumeration, with security-relevant roles (Admin, Security, Incident Response) specifically tracked. Process resources are validated by confirming the existence of governance procedures in the security-governance CodeCommit repository. Our small footprint enables complete boundary visibility across machine, human, and process resources.
results.justification: Validates the Live FedRAMP Boundary (MAS) across all three resource types: 1) Machine resources — the four critical asset classes (VPC boundary, EC2 compute, RDS data, S3 storage, ALB network), 2) Human resources — privileged IAM users/roles with production access, 3) Process resources — governance documentation defining security procedures.
results.pass_criteria: PASS if: A dedicated non-default VPC exists as the production boundary, at least one running EC2 instance operates within it, at least one RDS database instance is provisioned, S3 buckets exist for storage, at least one load balancer provides ingress/egress, IAM users and security-relevant roles are enumerated, and security governance procedures exist in CodeCommit.
results.fail_criteria: FAIL if: No non-default VPC exists, no running EC2 instances are found, no RDS instances are provisioned, no S3 buckets exist, no load balancers are configured, IAM enumeration fails, or security governance procedures are missing. Any missing resource class (machine, human, or process) means the Minimum Assessment Scope is incomplete.

results.requirement: Verify SCN procedures are documented and active change tracking is maintained.
results.implementation_summary: Significant Change Notifications are documented as code (governance/procedures/scn_procedures.md) and tracked by the Infrastructure SCN Monitor workflow, which classifies every infrastructure change per RFC-0007 and appends an immutable record to scn_automation/public_scn_history.jsonl on each run. All infrastructure changes require PR approval and generate this audit trail.
results.justification: Validates SCN evidence where it actually lives in this GitOps environment: the Significant Change Notification procedure is documented as code in governance/, and the change-tracking audit trail is the Infrastructure SCN Monitor's machine-readable history (scn_automation/public_scn_history.jsonl), regenerated and committed every run. Both are validated via the GitHub contents API — not an S3 drop-box.
results.pass_criteria: PASS if: the SCN procedure document exists with substantive content AND the SCN audit trail (public_scn_history.jsonl) exists with change records.
results.fail_criteria: FAIL if: the SCN procedure document is missing/empty, or the SCN audit trail is missing/empty.

results.requirement: Integrate FedRAMP's Incident Communications Procedures (ICP) into incident response procedures and infrastructure.

results.implementation_summary: Infrastructure changes execute exclusively through Terraform: version-controlled configuration, S3 state bucket (versioned) with DynamoDB state locking, peer-reviewed via GitHub pull requests. Application deployment to EC2 is currently a documented manual change-management procedure (see governance/procedures/change-management-procedure.md); automation via AWS Systems Manager (SSM) is on the roadmap and this KSI's evidence will incorporate SSM deployment execution data when it lands. No console changes permitted in production.
results.justification: Validates the version-controlled infrastructure change path (Terraform state bucket versioning + state locking), instance hardening (IMDSv2), and the documented configuration management policy. Deliberately does NOT claim CI/CD deployment metrics: application deployment is manual pending SSM automation, and the prior CodePipeline metric measured a defunct doc-sync pipeline unrelated to product delivery. Terraform state locking is enforced in the state-hosting AWS account (DynamoDB lock table co-located with the mks-states backend); it is not enumerable from the CSO account via dynamodb list-tables, so locking is evidenced by the documented backend configuration rather than a cross-account CLI call.
results.pass_criteria: PASS if: infrastructure changes are evidenced as version-controlled immutable redeployment — the SCN change history records Terraform-driven changes, running EC2 instances are present in the boundary, AND the configuration-management policy mandating Terraform-managed immutable infrastructure exists. (Terraform state versioning lives in the cross-account mks-states bucket; the version-controlled change path is evidenced git-natively.)
results.fail_criteria: FAIL if: No Terraform state bucket exists (infrastructure not managed as code), no DynamoDB lock table exists (no concurrency control), no managed EC2 instances exist, or the IaC policy document is missing.

command_executions.description: IMMUTABILITY: Validate the documented immutable-infrastructure methodology — all production changes via version-controlled Terraform (state in S3 with versioning + DynamoDB state locking, PR-reviewed plan/apply, change tracked in git history). The Terraform state itself lives in the [[s3-bucket]] bucket in a SEPARATE AWS account (cross-account, not readable by the validation role); the immutable-IaC methodology is evidenced as code in governance/. command_executions.description: FILTERED: Retrieve only IAM Users who have active password usage (Humans).
Verify that AWS Identity Center is the primary identity platform.
Get list of all Virtual MFA devices to validate protection (replaces list-mfa-devices which requires user).

Mode 2 (operational effectiveness) — cross-source correlator. Combines list-users (filtered to human users via PasswordLastUsed!=null), list-virtual-mfa-devices, and identitystore list-users (SCIM-provisioned AWS Identity Center users, which carry ExternalIds={Issuer,Id} only when federated from an external IdP such as Okta). IAMMFAMetricsPrimitive computes combined MFA coverage = SCIM-federated Identity Center users (MFA enforced upstream at the IdP) + local IAM console users with a virtual MFA device, over all human identities. SCIM credit is given ONLY for users whose collected data actually carries ExternalIds. KSI-IAM-01 target is 95% (FedRAMP IA-2(1); direct IdP MFA-policy verification pending the Okta API integration).
command_executions.command: echo '{}' | jq --argjson users "$(aws iam list-users --output json | jq '.Users // []')" --argjson mfa "$(aws iam list-virtual-mfa-devices --output json | jq '.VirtualMFADevices // []')" --argjson scim "$(aws identitystore list-users --identity-store-id $(aws sso-admin list-instances --query 'Instances[0].IdentityStoreId' --output text) --output json | jq '.Users // []')" '{HumanUsers: ($users | map(select(.PasswordLastUsed != null))), VirtualMFADevices: $mfa, ScimUsers: $scim}'

results.implementation_summary: Primary authentication via AWS Identity Center (SSO). Fallback IAM password policy enforces 14+ chars, complexity, 90-day rotation.

results.implementation_summary: Centralized identity via AWS Identity Center. Legacy IAM users audited and minimized (target: 0 human IAM users).
results.justification: Validates the presence of the Centralized Identity Provider (Identity Center) and audits the 'Debt' of decentralized local IAM users.

results.fail_criteria: FAIL if: No Identity Center instance exists, or multiple IAM users have active console passwords indicating widespread bypass of centralized identity management.

results.requirement: Persistently ensure that identity and access management employs measures to ensure each user or device can only access the resources they need.
results.implementation_summary: Phishing-resistant MFA enforced via AWS Identity Center with FIDO2/WebAuthn support. Virtual MFA devices audited to identify non-compliant tokens. IAM password policy provides hard-fail backstop if MFA is missing.
results.justification: Validates that the authentication flow prevents phishing by enforcing FIDO2/WebAuthn at the Identity Provider level (SSO) and ensuring break-glass users utilize hardware tokens.
results.pass_criteria: PASS if: AWS Identity Center is configured with FIDO2/WebAuthn support, all MFA devices are inventoried (preference for hardware/FIDO tokens over virtual), AND the IAM password policy provides a hard-fail backstop if MFA is missing.
results.fail_criteria: FAIL if: Identity Center is not configured, MFA device inventory shows unprotected accounts, or no password policy exists as fallback protection.
results.cli_command: aws sso-admin list-instances --output json; aws iam list-virtual-mfa-devices --output json; aws iam get-account-password-policy --output json || echo '{"PasswordPolicy": null}'

command_executions.description: PRIMARY: Validate existence and configuration of the CloudTrail trail.

STATUS: Confirm CloudTrail is actually '[resource]': true. Uses TrailARN to avoid naming conflicts. Mode 2 (operational effectiveness) — TrailLoggingMetricsPrimitive computes logging_rate as % of trails with IsLogging=true; KSI-MLA-01 target is 100%.
command_executions.command: aws cloudtrail get-trail-status --name $(aws cloudtrail describe-trails --query 'trailList[0].TrailARN' --output text) --output json

command_executions.description: STORAGE: Validate the specific S3 bucket used for audit log retention.

Mode 2 — S3LoggingCoverageMetrics computes logging_rate as % of S3 buckets with server access logging enabled (vector 6). Target 100%. KSI-MLA-01's outcome (log all activity) requires every bucket to log; gaps are blind spots for object-read attacks.
command_executions.command: ( for b in $(aws s3api [s3-bucket] --query 'Buckets[].Name' --output text 2>/dev/null); do (aws s3api [s3-bucket] --bucket "$b" --output json 2>/dev/null || echo '{}') | jq --arg b "$b" '. + {Bucket: $b}'; done ) | jq -s '{BucketLoggingStatus: .}'

command_executions.description: Mode 2 — ALBAccessLogCoverageMetrics computes logging_rate as % of ALBs exporting access_logs.s3 to S3 (vectors 5, 6). Without ALB access logs, request-level visibility into the LMS edge depends solely on application logs (which an attacker may suppress). Target 100%.

command_executions.description: LOGS: Validate that CloudTrail Log File Validation is enabled (Cryptographic hashing of audit trails). Uses describe-trails to enumerate trail ARNs (including shadow entries for organization trails owned by other accounts), then calls get-trail per ARN — get-trail returns the FULL Trail object including LogFileValidationEnabled even for shadow trails, where describe-trails strips that field from the home-account-controlled view on member-account responses. Required when the boundary delegates audit logging to an org trail in a separate management account.

results.requirement: Validates the complete FedRAMP Minimum Assessment Scope (MAS) covering machine resources, human resources, and process resources. This rule enforces Structural Boundaries (VPC ID) for compute assets, validates peripheral assets (S3, RDS, ALB), enumerates privileged human access (IAM), and confirms governance processes exist.
results.implementation_summary: Meridian maintains a single-VPC architecture with all production resources (5 EC2 instances, 1 RDS database, 11 S3 buckets, 1 ALB) explicitly enumerated and validated daily. Human resources are inventoried via IAM user and role enumeration, with security-relevant roles (Admin, Security, Incident Response) specifically tracked. Process resources are validated by confirming the existence of governance procedures in the security-governance CodeCommit repository. Our small footprint enables complete boundary visibility across machine, human, and process resources.
results.justification: Validates the Live FedRAMP Boundary (MAS) across all three resource types: 1) Machine resources — the four critical asset classes (VPC boundary, EC2 compute, RDS data, S3 storage, ALB network), 2) Human resources — privileged IAM users/roles with production access, 3) Process resources — governance documentation defining security procedures.
results.pass_criteria: PASS if: A dedicated non-default VPC exists as the production boundary, at least one running EC2 instance operates within it, at least one RDS database instance is provisioned, S3 buckets exist for storage, at least one load balancer provides ingress/egress, IAM users and security-relevant roles are enumerated, and security governance procedures exist in CodeCommit.
results.fail_criteria: FAIL if: No non-default VPC exists, no running EC2 instances are found, no RDS instances are provisioned, no S3 buckets exist, no load balancers are configured, IAM enumeration fails, or security governance procedures are missing. Any missing resource class (machine, human, or process) means the Minimum Assessment Scope is incomplete.

Deliberately does NOT claim CI/CD deployment metrics: application deployment is manual pending SSM automation, and the prior CodePipeline metric measured a defunct doc-sync pipeline unrelated to product delivery. Terraform state locking is enforced in the state-hosting AWS account (DynamoDB lock table co-located with the mks-states backend); it is not enumerable from the CSO account via dynamodb list-tables, so locking is evidenced by the documented backend configuration rather than a cross-account CLI call.

command_executions.description: FILTERED: Retrieve only IAM Users who have active password usage (Humans).
Verify that AWS Identity Center is the primary identity platform.
Get list of all Virtual MFA devices to validate protection (replaces list-mfa-devices which requires user).

Mode 2 (operational effectiveness) — cross-source correlator. Combines list-users (filtered to human users via PasswordLastUsed!=null), list-virtual-mfa-devices, and identitystore list-users (SCIM-provisioned AWS Identity Center users, which carry ExternalIds={Issuer,Id} only when federated from an external IdP such as Okta). IAMMFAMetricsPrimitive computes combined MFA coverage = SCIM-federated Identity Center users (MFA enforced upstream at the IdP) + local IAM console users with a virtual MFA device, over all human identities. SCIM credit is given ONLY for users whose collected data actually carries ExternalIds. KSI-IAM-01 target is 95% (FedRAMP IA-2(1); direct IdP MFA-policy verification pending the Okta API integration).
command_executions.command: echo '{}' | jq --argjson users "$(aws iam list-users --output json | jq '.Users // []')" --argjson mfa "$(aws iam list-virtual-mfa-devices --output json | jq '.VirtualMFADevices // []')" --argjson scim "$(aws identitystore list-users --identity-store-id $(aws sso-admin list-instances --query 'Instances[0].IdentityStoreId' --output text) --output json | jq '.Users // []')" '{HumanUsers: ($users | map(select(.PasswordLastUsed != null))), VirtualMFADevices: $mfa, ScimUsers: $scim}'

command_executions.description: Mode 2 (operational effectiveness) — SSOSessionDurationMetrics computes within_target_rate as % of permission sets with SessionDuration <= 8h. Long-lived sessions extend the LMS session-theft attack window (vector 1). Target 100%.
command_executions.command: ( IN=$(aws sso-admin list-instances --query 'Instances[0].InstanceArn' --output text 2>/dev/null); for ps in $(aws sso-admin list-permission-sets --instance-arn "$IN" --query 'PermissionSets[]' --output text 2>/dev/null); do aws sso-admin describe-permission-set --instance-arn "$IN" --permission-set-arn "$ps" --output json 2>/dev/null | jq '.PermissionSet'; done ) | jq -s '{PermissionSets: .}'

results.requirement: Use secure passwordless methods for user authentication and authorization when feasible, otherwise enforce strong passwords with MFA.
results.implementation_summary: Primary authentication via AWS Identity Center (SSO). Fallback IAM password policy enforces 14+ chars, complexity, 90-day rotation.
results.justification: Validates the authentication hierarchy: Checks for Identity Center (Best) and SAML Providers (Good) first. If those are absent, it validates the IAM Password Policy (Fallback) to ensure strong controls are in place.
results.pass_criteria: PASS if: AWS Identity Center (SSO) is configured as the primary authentication method. If SAML providers exist, they provide federated access. If IAM passwords are used as fallback, the password policy enforces minimum 14 characters, complexity requirements, and 90-day rotation.
results.fail_criteria: FAIL if: No Identity Center instance exists AND no SAML providers are configured (no centralized auth), or the IAM password policy allows passwords shorter than 14 characters or lacks complexity/rotation requirements.

results.requirement: Enforce appropriately secure authentication methods for non-user accounts and services.
results.implementation_summary: Service accounts use IAM Roles with no long-lived credentials. Access keys (where required) rotate every 90 days per credential report monitoring.
results.justification: Validates the security of machine identities. Filters out AWS-managed roles to focus strictly on Customer Roles (Trust Policies) and Service Accounts (Access Keys).

results.requirement: Validates the complete FedRAMP Minimum Assessment Scope (MAS) covering machine resources, human resources, and process resources. This rule enforces Structural Boundaries (VPC ID) for compute assets, validates peripheral assets (S3, RDS, ALB), enumerates privileged human access (IAM), and confirms governance processes exist.
results.implementation_summary: Meridian maintains a single-VPC architecture with all production resources (5 EC2 instances, 1 RDS database, 11 S3 buckets, 1 ALB) explicitly enumerated and validated daily. Human resources are inventoried via IAM user and role enumeration, with security-relevant roles (Admin, Security, Incident Response) specifically tracked. Process resources are validated by confirming the existence of governance procedures in the security-governance CodeCommit repository. Our small footprint enables complete boundary visibility across machine, human, and process resources.
results.justification: Validates the Live FedRAMP Boundary (MAS) across all three resource types: 1) Machine resources — the four critical asset classes (VPC boundary, EC2 compute, RDS data, S3 storage, ALB network), 2) Human resources — privileged IAM users/roles with production access, 3) Process resources — governance documentation defining security procedures.
results.pass_criteria: PASS if: A dedicated non-default VPC exists as the production boundary, at least one running EC2 instance operates within it, at least one RDS database instance is provisioned, S3 buckets exist for storage, at least one load balancer provides ingress/egress, IAM users and security-relevant roles are enumerated, and security governance procedures exist in CodeCommit.
results.fail_criteria: FAIL if: No non-default VPC exists, no running EC2 instances are found, no RDS instances are provisioned, no S3 buckets exist, no load balancers are configured, IAM enumeration fails, or security governance procedures are missing. Any missing resource class (machine, human, or process) means the Minimum Assessment Scope is incomplete.

results.requirement: Execute changes to machine-based information resources through redeployment of version controlled immutable resources rather than direct modification wherever possible.
results.implementation_summary: Infrastructure changes execute exclusively through Terraform: version-controlled configuration, S3 state bucket (versioned) with DynamoDB state locking, peer-reviewed via GitHub pull requests. Application deployment to EC2 is currently a documented manual change-management procedure (see governance/procedures/change-management-procedure.md); automation via AWS Systems Manager (SSM) is on the roadmap and this KSI's evidence will incorporate SSM deployment execution data when it lands. No console changes permitted in production.
results.justification: Validates the version-controlled infrastructure change path (Terraform state bucket versioning + state locking), instance hardening (IMDSv2), and the documented configuration management policy. Deliberately does NOT claim CI/CD deployment metrics: application deployment is manual pending SSM automation, and the prior CodePipeline metric measured a defunct doc-sync pipeline unrelated to product delivery. Terraform state locking is enforced in the state-hosting AWS account (DynamoDB lock table co-located with the mks-states backend); it is not enumerable from the CSO account via dynamodb list-tables, so locking is evidenced by the documented backend configuration rather than a cross-account CLI call.
results.pass_criteria: PASS if: infrastructure changes are evidenced as version-controlled immutable redeployment — the SCN change history records Terraform-driven changes, running EC2 instances are present in the boundary, AND the configuration-management policy mandating Terraform-managed immutable infrastructure exists. (Terraform state versioning lives in the cross-account mks-states bucket; the version-controlled change path is evidenced git-natively.)
results.fail_criteria: FAIL if: No Terraform state bucket exists (infrastructure not managed as code), no DynamoDB lock table exists (no concurrency control), no managed EC2 instances exist, or the IaC policy document is missing.

results.justification: Validates Logical Traffic Enforcement. Checks for: 1) VPC Endpoints (Enforces private connectivity to AWS services), 2) NAT Gateways (Enforces centralized egress), and 3) Load Balancer Schemes (Enforces defined entry points).
results.pass_criteria: PASS if: At least one VPC endpoint exists (enforcing private connectivity to AWS services like S3/DynamoDB), at least one NAT gateway is available (centralized egress for private subnets), AND at least one load balancer manages ingress traffic.
results.fail_criteria: FAIL if: No VPC endpoints exist (traffic to AWS services traverses public internet), no NAT gateway is available, or no load balancer exists for ingress management.
results.cli_command: aws ec2 [vpc]-endpoints --output json; aws ec2 describe-nat-gateways --filter Name=state,Values=available --output json; aws elbv2 describe-load-balancers --output json

results.requirement: Use immutable infrastructure with strictly defined functionality and privileges by default.
results.implementation_summary: Immutable infrastructure via Terraform. EC2 instances replaced (not modified) on updates. IAM roles follow least-privilege with no wildcard permissions.
results.justification: Validates Immutable EC2 Infrastructure & Least Privilege. Checks for: 1) Terraform State (Proof of immutable IaC management), 2) Security Groups (Strict network functionality), and 3) Custom IAM Roles (Strictly defined privileges, excluding default AWS service roles).
results.pass_criteria: PASS if: A Terraform state file exists in S3 (proving IaC management), no security groups allow unrestricted 'All Traffic' protocol (-1) to 0.0.0.0/0, AND at least one custom IAM role exists following least-privilege principles (excluding AWS service-linked roles).
results.fail_criteria: FAIL if: No Terraform state exists (infrastructure not immutable), any security group allows 'All Traffic' to 0.0.0.0/0, or no custom IAM roles exist.

command_executions.description: IMMUTABILITY: Validate the documented immutable-infrastructure methodology — all production changes via version-controlled Terraform (state in S3 with versioning + DynamoDB state locking, PR-reviewed plan/apply, change tracked in git history). The Terraform state itself lives in the [[s3-bucket]] bucket in a SEPARATE AWS account (cross-account, not readable by the validation role); the immutable-IaC methodology is evidenced as code in governance/. [Policy-as-code home: governance/ in this git repository; validated via GitHub contents API.]
FUNCTIONALITY: Check for Security Groups allowing 'All Traffic' (-1). Absence of these proves strictly defined network functionality.
PRIVILEGE: Audit Custom IAM Roles. This focuses the scan on roles you created, verifying they follow least-privilege principles.

results.requirement: Persistently review the effectiveness of protection against denial of service attacks and other unwanted activity.
results.implementation_summary: DDoS protection via AWS Shield Standard (automatic on ALB), WAFv2 Web ACLs, and Route53 anycast. SES handles email with DKIM/SPF validation.
results.justification: Validates DDoS & Spoofing Defense. Checks for: 1) Regional WAF (Layer 7 Defense), 2) Load Balancers (Layer 3/4 Defense via AWS Shield Standard), and 3) Validated Email Identities (Spoofing protection via SES/DKIM).
results.pass_criteria: PASS if: At least one WAFv2 Web ACL exists (Layer 7 protection), at least one load balancer with Shield Standard provides Layer 3/4 protection, at least one Route53 hosted zone exists (DNS resilience), AND if SES is used, verified identities exist with DKIM/SPF.
results.fail_criteria: FAIL if: No WAF Web ACLs exist, no load balancers provide Shield protection, or no Route53 hosted zones exist for DNS resilience.
results.cli_command: aws wafv2 list-web-acls --scope REGIONAL --output json; aws elbv2 describe-load-balancers --query "LoadBalancers[].{ARN:LoadBalancerArn, DNS:DNSName, Type:Type, Scheme:Scheme}" --output json; aws route53 list-hosted-zones --max-items 5 --output json; aws ses list-identities --max-items 5 --output json; ( for id in $(aws apigateway get-rest-apis --query 'items[].id' --output text 2>/dev/null); do aws apigateway get-stages --rest-api-id "$id" --output json 2>/dev/null | jq --arg id "$id" '(.item // []) | map(. + {restApiId: $id})'; done ) | jq -s '{Stages: [.[][]?]}'

resource_evaluations.reason: WAF protection active: 1 Web ACL(s) configured for Layer 7 defense.
1 load balancer(s) covered by AWS Shield Standard — automatic, always-on Layer 3/4 DDoS protection for all AWS load balancers (no subscription required; AWS baseline).
DNS resilience: 1 Route53 hosted zone(s) on AWS AnyCast network.

3 resource ID(s) returned (SES identities should exist if email is used (validates DKIM/SPF anti-spoofing)) — inventory context only; IDs do not evidence the security property. Add a describe-* command to verify.

command_executions.description: DDOS LAYER 7: Validate existence of Web Application Firewalls (WAF) to block malicious requests.

DDOS LAYER 3/4 (Shield): Validate Load Balancers. AWS Shield Standard provides automatic, always-on Layer 3/4 DDoS protection for every ALB/NLB at no cost — load balancers ARE the protected perimeter. (No per-resource subscription exists for Shield Standard; this is the AWS baseline.)
command_executions.command: aws elbv2 describe-load-balancers --query "LoadBalancers[].{ARN:LoadBalancerArn, DNS:DNSName, Type:Type, Scheme:Scheme}" --output json

command_executions.description: DNS RESILIENCE: Validate Route53 usage (AWS AnyCast network provides DNS flood protection).
SPOOFING: Validate SES Identities. If email is sent, this checks for authorized identities (DKIM/SPF managed).

Mode 2 — APIThrottleCoverageMetrics computes throttle_rate as % of API Gateway stages with non-zero throttle limits configured (vector 5). Without per-stage throttling, a valid token can be used to enumerate /api/v1/* at line rate. Target 100%.

results.implementation_summary: High availability via Multi-AZ ALB, Multi-AZ RDS, and AWS Backup plans with 24-hour RPO. RTO target: 4 hours for full recovery.

command_executions.description: FILTERED: Retrieve only IAM Users who have active password usage (Humans).
Verify that AWS Identity Center is the primary identity platform.

Mode 2 (operational effectiveness) — cross-source correlator. Combines list-users (filtered to human users via PasswordLastUsed!=null), list-virtual-mfa-devices, and identitystore list-users (SCIM-provisioned AWS Identity Center users, which carry ExternalIds={Issuer,Id} only when federated from an external IdP such as Okta). IAMMFAMetricsPrimitive computes combined MFA coverage = SCIM-federated Identity Center users (MFA enforced upstream at the IdP) + local IAM console users with a virtual MFA device, over all human identities. SCIM credit is given ONLY for users whose collected data actually carries ExternalIds. KSI-IAM-01 target is 95% (FedRAMP IA-2(1); direct IdP MFA-policy verification pending the Okta API integration).

results.requirement: Automatically disable or otherwise secure accounts with privileged access in response to suspicious activity
results.implementation_summary: Automated response to suspicious activity via GuardDuty → EventBridge → Lambda chain. High-severity findings trigger automatic credential rotation.

results.fail_criteria: FAIL if: The access review policy document is missing from CodeCommit, or no EventBridge rules exist to monitor IAM events (no automated access review capability).

Uses describe-trails to enumerate trail ARNs (including shadow entries for organization trails owned by other accounts), then calls get-trail per ARN — get-trail returns the FULL Trail object including LogFileValidationEnabled even for shadow trails, where describe-trails strips that field from the home-account-controlled view on member-account responses. Required when the boundary delegates audit logging to an org trail in a separate management account.
